summary: In Book I of the Nicomachean Ethics, Aristotle investigates the highest human good, identifying it as happiness (eudaimonia). He argues that happiness is not found in mere pleasure, wealth, or honor, but in the active exercise of the soul's rational faculties in accordance with virtue, sustained over a complete life, and supported by adequate external goods.

principles.statement: Every action and choice aims at some good.
principles.rationale: All human endeavors, arts, and inquiries are purposeful and directed toward an end, which ultimately culminates in a highest good.
principles.source_quote: Every art and every inquiry, and similarly every action as well as choice, is held to aim at some good.

principles.statement: Do not seek more precision than the subject matter allows.
principles.rationale: Ethics and politics deal with variable human actions and conventions, not absolute mathematical certainties. Demanding strict proofs in these fields is a category error.
principles.source_quote: For it belongs to an educated person to seek out precision in each genus to the extent that the nature of the matter allows: to accept persuasive speech from a skilled mathematician appears comparable to demanding demonstrations from a skilled rhetorician.

principles.statement: The human good is an activity of the soul in accord with virtue.
principles.rationale: A human being's unique function is the active life of reason. Performing this function well and nobly constitutes the highest human good.
principles.source_quote: ...the human good becomes an activity of soul in accord with virtue, and if there are several virtues, then in accord with the best and most complete one.

principles.rationale: It is impossible or very difficult to perform noble actions without resources like friends, wealth, or political power, and severe misfortunes can disfigure one's blessedness.
principles.source_quote: Nonetheless, it manifestly requires external goods in addition, just as we said. For it is impossible or not easy for someone without equipment to do what is noble...

frameworks.name: The Function (Ergon) Argument
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to determine the specific good, excellence, or purpose of a being, profession, or object.
steps: Identify the subject's unique work or function, setting aside functions shared with lower forms of life (e.g., nutrition, mere perception).
Determine the active exercise of that specific function (for humans, the active life of reason).
Evaluate the performance of that function 'well and nobly' in accordance with its proper virtue.
frameworks.source_quote: For just as in the case of an aulos player, sculptor, and every expert, and in general with those who have a certain work and action, the relevant good and the doing of something well seem to reside in the work, so too the same might be held to be the case with a human being, if in fact there is a certain work that is a human being's.

mental_models.name: Architectonic Hierarchy of Ends
mental_models.description: Viewing goals and arts as a nested hierarchy where subordinate arts serve higher, 'architectonic' arts, culminating in a master art that dictates the ultimate end.
mental_models.example: Aristotle uses this to show that bridle making serves horsemanship, which serves generalship, which ultimately falls under the political art, making the political art's end the overarching human good.

mental_models.name: The Archer and the Target
mental_models.description: A metaphor for purposeful living. Knowing the ultimate good gives us a specific target to aim at, drastically increasing our chances of hitting what is needed rather than acting aimlessly.
mental_models.example: And with a view to our life, then, is not the knowledge of this good of great weight, and would we not, like archers in possession of a target, better hit on what is needed?

heuristics: One swallow does not make a spring, nor does one day. (Judge happiness over a complete life, not a fleeting moment.)
Begin from what is known to us, rather than what is known simply.

signature_quotes.text: Every art and every inquiry, and similarly every action as well as choice, is held to aim at some good.
signature_quotes.context: The opening line of the Nicomachean Ethics, establishing the teleological nature of human action.

signature_quotes.text: For one swallow does not make a spring, nor does one day. And in this way, one day or a short time does not make someone blessed and happy either.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that happiness requires a complete life, not just a brief period of virtue or success.

signature_quotes.text: For it belongs to an educated person to seek out precision in each genus to the extent that the nature of the matter allows...
signature_quotes.context: Warning against demanding mathematical exactness in the study of ethics and politics.

signature_quotes.text: ...the human good becomes an activity of soul in accord with virtue, and if there are several virtues, then in accord with the best and most complete one.
signature_quotes.context: The conclusion of the function argument, defining the essence of human happiness.

anti_patterns.description: Equating happiness with wealth, pleasure, or honor.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Wealth is merely a means to an end; honor depends on those who bestow it rather than the recipient; and a life of mere pleasure is fit for 'fatted cattle' and is slavish.

anti_patterns.description: Demanding mathematical demonstrations in political and ethical arguments.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The noble and just things examined by the political art admit of much dispute and variability, making absolute precision impossible and inappropriate.

anti_patterns.description: Teaching political science to the young or immature.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They lack experience in life's actions and are driven by passions, making the knowledge useless since the goal of ethics is action, not mere knowledge.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in a single, universal Platonic 'Idea' of the Good.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: 'Good' is spoken of in many different categories (time, place, relation) and a separate, abstract 'Good-as-such' cannot be possessed or acted upon by a human being.
